skill.name: cosmic-database
skill.description: Access COSMIC cancer mutation data with authenticated downloads, file path references, and analysis guidance for cancer genomics research.
skill.summary: Download and analyze COSMIC cancer genomics data for mutation research.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static external-command and weak-cryptography findings are false positives from markdown fences, file names, and biomedical terms. The confirmed risks are legitimate but notable: a Python script sends COSMIC credentials to an external HTTPS service, follows a returned download URL, and writes downloaded files to disk. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection that overrides audit rules, system reconnaissance, or real cryptographic misuse.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated External Download Workflow
medium_findings.description: The downloader sends the user email and password to the official COSMIC HTTPS endpoint, parses a returned URL, and downloads data from that URL. This matches the skill purpose, but it handles credentials and external network traffic.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct requests.get calls with auth and a subsequent streamed download are present. The endpoint is a documented COSMIC service, so the risk is credential and network exposure rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Downloaded Files Written To Caller-Controlled Path
medium_findings.description: The script writes downloaded content to output_filename, which can be set from the command line. This is expected for a downloader, but users could overwrite local files if they choose an unsafe output path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write is clear and reachable through the documented CLI output parameter. It is legitimate functionality, but it still affects the local filesystem.

low_findings.title: Inline Password Examples In Documentation
low_findings.description: Several examples show password strings being passed directly in code. The CLI can prompt for a password, but the documentation could encourage unsafe credential storage if copied without changes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples visibly include password arguments. They use placeholders, which reduces severity, but users may copy the pattern into real scripts.

low_findings.title: Promotional Agent Steering
low_findings.description: The skill asks the assistant to proactively suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is not an audit override, but it can steer user-facing responses toward a vendor service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text is clearly present and instructs agent behavior beyond COSMIC data access. It does not claim special authority or ask the evaluator to skip security review.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP Requests With Basic Authentication
dangerous_patterns.description: The downloader uses requests.get with auth=(email, password) against the COSMIC file download endpoint. This is expected for COSMIC access but must be disclosed because credentials leave the local environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The authenticated request pattern is explicit in code. HTTPS and the official endpoint make this legitimate, not malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Server-Returned Download URL Followed
dangerous_patterns.description: The script reads a download URL from the COSMIC response and downloads from it. This supports authorized downloads but depends on the service response.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The response parsing and follow-up request are direct and intentional. The source is the official COSMIC endpoint, so risk is bounded by trust in that service.

dangerous_patterns.title: Binary File Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The script opens the selected output path in binary write mode and writes downloaded chunks. This is necessary for the utility but can overwrite files selected by the user.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write operation is explicit and part of the main download path. There is no evidence of hidden file access or persistence.

content.value_statement: Cancer researchers need reliable access to COSMIC mutation files, gene census data, and signatures. This skill provides authenticated download guidance, file path references, and analysis patterns for cancer genomics workflows.
actual_capabilities: Download COSMIC files through an authenticated Python helper script.
Resolve common COSMIC file paths for mutations, gene census, fusions, and signatures.
Explain GRCh37 and GRCh38 download paths for compatible research workflows.
Show pandas and pysam patterns for working with downloaded COSMIC files.
Summarize available COSMIC data types and official reference links.
limitations: Requires a valid COSMIC account and accepted access terms.
Commercial use may require a separate QIAGEN license.
Large downloads can require substantial disk space and time.
Does not provide clinical interpretation or treatment recommendations.

use_cases.title: Prepare mutation datasets
use_cases.description: Find the correct COSMIC files and download coding mutation data for a research cohort.

use_cases.title: Build data pipelines
use_cases.description: Integrate COSMIC downloads and file path shortcuts into reproducible analysis workflows.

use_cases.title: Review cancer gene evidence
use_cases.description: Use Cancer Gene Census and resistance mutation files to support research review.

prompt_templates.title: Find a COSMIC file
prompt_templates.prompt: Which COSMIC file should I use for the Cancer Gene Census, and what access steps are required?

prompt_templates.title: Download mutation data
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through downloading COSMIC coding mutation data for GRCh38 using the helper script.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a downloaded file
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how to load a downloaded COSMIC mutation table and filter records for TP53 in lung cancer.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a reproducible pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a reproducible workflow that downloads COSMIC gene census, mutation, and fusion files for quarterly updates.

output_examples.input: I need COSMIC Cancer Gene Census data for a new GRCh38 project.
output: Use the Cancer Gene Census CSV path for GRCh38 with the latest release marker.
Confirm your COSMIC account access before downloading.
Read the downloaded CSV with a table tool such as pandas for role and tier filtering.

output_examples.input: I want to compare mutation records for a single gene.
output: Download the coding mutation export for the target genome assembly.
Load the compressed table locally after the download completes.
Filter by the gene symbol column and then stratify by primary site or histology.

output_examples.input: I need a safe way to run a large COSMIC download.
output: Use the password prompt instead of storing credentials in source files.
Choose an output directory with enough free disk space.
Record the COSMIC version and genome assembly in your workflow notes.

best_practices: Use the password prompt or a secure secret manager instead of hardcoding credentials.
Record the COSMIC release version and genome assembly for reproducibility.
Download only the data types needed for the current research question.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real COSMIC passwords into notebooks, prompts, or shared scripts.
Do not assume commercial rights without checking the COSMIC license terms.
Do not mix GRCh37 and GRCh38 files without an explicit liftover plan.

faq.question: Does this skill include COSMIC data?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and a helper script for downloading data from COSMIC after authentication.

faq.question: Do I need a COSMIC account?
faq.answer: Yes. COSMIC downloads require registration, and commercial users may need a separate license.

faq.question: Which genome assembly should I choose?
faq.answer: Use GRCh38 for new projects. Use GRCh37 only when an existing workflow requires it.

faq.question: Can this skill interpret patient results?
faq.answer: No. It supports research data access and analysis planning, not clinical diagnosis or treatment advice.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The helper script uses network access, handles COSMIC credentials, and writes downloaded files to disk.

faq.question: What tools can read the downloaded files?
faq.answer: The documentation references pandas for tables and pysam for VCF files, depending on the file format.
